county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Río Grande, established 1840, carved from Loíza. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Río Grande was carved from Loíza in 1840 on the northeast coast at the base of El Yunque. Parroquia Nuestra Señora del Carmen holds records from the 1840s. Pre-1840 records are in Loíza's parish archives. The El Yunque rainforest boundary runs through the municipio — US Forest Service records at NARA from 1903 supplement local sources. Sugar plantations along the Río Grande de Loíza generated enslaved persons records. The resort development at Río Mar and Palmer areas created modern property records. Barrios like Ciénaga Alta, Guzmán Abajo/Arriba, and Jiménez have distinct colonial-era records. Check Loíza's important Afro-Puerto Rican parish registers for pre-1840 families.

source_profiles.source_name: Río Grande Catholic Parish Registers (Baptisms, Marriages, Burials)
source_profiles.repository: Parish Archives / FamilySearch / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic parish registers for Río Grande municipio including baptisms (bautismos), marriages (matrimonios), and burials (defunciones). FamilySearch has digitized microfilm for many Puerto Rico parishes. Coverage begins circa 1840.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Parish registers are the primary genealogical source for Puerto Rico. Some early volumes deteriorated due to tropical climate. FamilySearch microfilm provides backup copies.

source_profiles.source_name: Río Grande Civil Registry (Registro Demográfico) — Births, Marriages, Deaths
source_profiles.repository: Registro Demográfico, Río Grande / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil registry records for Río Grande including births, marriages, and deaths from 1885. Spanish civil registration began 1885 under Royal Decree. US administration continued the system after 1898.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Civil registry records generally well-preserved from 1885. Some gaps in early years due to incomplete compliance. Tropical climate and hurricanes have affected some volumes.

source_profiles.source_name: Río Grande Notarial Protocols (Protocolos Notariales)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Notarial protocols for Río Grande district covering property transfers, testaments, powers of attorney, marriage contracts, slave sales, coartación agreements, and other legal instruments from 1840. Spanish legal tradition required notarized documents for most transactions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Notarial protocols at AGPR are generally well-preserved. Some volumes from the earliest periods show deterioration. AGPR's climate-controlled storage protects these irreplaceable records.

source_profiles.source_name: Río Grande Spanish Colonial Census Records (Padrones)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan / Archivo Histórico Nacional, Madrid
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Spanish colonial census records (padrones) for Río Grande. Include population counts by race, age, occupation, and household. Key padrones: 1765, 1776, 1800, 1812, 1820, 1846, 1860, 1887, 1897. Some padrones list individuals by name; others are statistical summaries.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Survival varies by year. The 1800, 1860, and 1897 padrones are among the most complete. Some early padrones survive only as summaries at the Archivo Histórico Nacional in Madrid.

source_profiles.source_name: Río Grande Property Registry (Registro de la Propiedad)
source_profiles.repository: Registro de la Propiedad, Río Grande district
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Property registry records for Río Grande district. The Spanish mortgage law system (Ley Hipotecaria) was established in Puerto Rico in 1870, creating a formal property registration system that continues today. Records document property ownership, transfers, mortgages, and liens.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Property registry records are generally well-preserved from 1870. The formal registration system provides continuous chains of title.

source_profiles.source_name: Río Grande Municipal Cemetery Records (Cementerio Municipal)
source_profiles.repository: Municipio de Río Grande / Find A Grave
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Municipal cemetery records for Río Grande. Municipal cemeteries were established in most Puerto Rico municipios during the 19th century under Spanish health regulations. Records include burial registers, grave assignments, and monument inscriptions. Find A Grave has partial crowdsourced transcriptions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Municipal cemetery records vary in completeness. Earlier burials were in church cemeteries (camposantos). Some municipal cemetery records were damaged by hurricanes.

source_profiles.source_name: Río Grande Slave Registers and Abolition Records (1872-1873)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Slave registers (registros de esclavos) for Río Grande including the 1872 census of enslaved persons mandated before the 1873 Abolition Law. Also includes coartación (self-purchase) agreements, liberto (freed persons) records, and slave sale documents in notarial protocols. Essential for tracing African-descended families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: The 1872 slave register is generally well-preserved at AGPR. Earlier slave records are scattered across notarial protocols, church registers, and estate inventories.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Sabana Grande, established 1813, carved from San Germán. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Sabana Grande was carved from San Germán in 1813 in the southwest foothills. Known for its Pozo de la Virgen (miracle well). Parroquia San Isidro Labrador holds records from the early 19th century. Pre-1813 records are in San Germán, one of Puerto Rico's two founding towns. Coffee cultivation in the highland barrios and sugar along the coastal plain created dual agricultural record sets at AGPR. The religious pilgrimage tradition associated with the miracle well means church records include visitors from across the island. Corsican immigration in the 19th century is documented in passenger records. Check San Germán's extensive colonial archives for pre-separation families.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Salinas, established 1851, carved from Coamo, Guayama. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Salinas was carved from Coamo and Guayama in 1851 on the south-central coast. Known for its salt flats and seafood. Parroquia Nuestra Señora de la Monserrate holds records from the 1850s. Pre-1851 records divide between Coamo (one of PR's oldest parishes) and Guayama. Sugar plantations generated extensive enslaved persons records. Camp Santiago military base (established 1940) created military personnel records at NARA. Fishing village records at Playa de Salinas provide maritime genealogical data. The salt industry (salinas) created distinctive occupational records. Check both Coamo and Guayama parish archives — Salinas territory was split between these ancient jurisdictions.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of San Germán, established 1573, one of Puerto Rico's original settlements. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: San Germán, founded in 1573, is Puerto Rico's second-oldest European settlement and was the only other town besides San Juan for over a century. Parroquia San Germán de Auxerre holds some of the island's oldest church records, with registers from the 1500s-1600s. The Porta Coeli church (1606) is one of the oldest in the Americas. As parent to Cabo Rojo, Sabana Grande, Lajas, Añasco, Maricao, and indirectly many others, San Germán's records are foundational for all of western Puerto Rico. The Archivo Histórico de San Germán is a critical research repository. Spanish colonial censuses, notarial protocols, and ecclesiastical records here predate most other island jurisdictions.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of San Juan, established 1521, one of Puerto Rico's original settlements. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: San Juan, founded in 1521, is Puerto Rico's capital and oldest city. The Cathedral of San Juan Bautista holds the island's oldest church records — baptisms, marriages, and burials from the 1500s. As the colonial capital, San Juan's archives are unparalleled: the Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR) holds notarial protocols, government correspondence, military records, and civil registry. The Archivo Histórico Municipal documents city governance. The fortifications (El Morro, San Cristóbal) generated military records spanning four centuries. Immigration records, customs house records, and port records document arrivals from Spain, Africa, and the Americas. The University of Puerto Rico's Centro de Investigaciones Históricas holds essential manuscript collections.

source_profiles.repository: Colección Puertorriqueña, UPR / Biblioteca Nacional / Chronicling America

Papers include birth/death/marriage announcements, legal notices, estate settlements, and community news. La Gaceta de Puerto Rico (official gazette) published government notices.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Some newspaper runs are incomplete. The Colección Puertorriqueña at UPR Río Piedras has the most extensive holdings. Chronicling America has digitized selected Puerto Rico newspapers.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of San Lorenzo, established 1811, carved from Caguas, Humacao. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: San Lorenzo was carved from Caguas and Humacao in 1811 in the eastern interior. Known as 'La Ciudad del Sapo' and 'La Cuna de la Trova.' Parroquia Nuestra Señora de las Mercedes holds records from the early 19th century. Pre-1811 records divide between Caguas and Humacao parishes. Tobacco was the primary crop — cigar factory and tobacco warehouse records at AGPR document working families. The trova music tradition creates cultural documentation unique to San Lorenzo. Barrios like Cerro Gordo, Espino, Florida, Hato, and Quemados have distinct settlement histories. Check both Caguas and Humacao church registers for pre-1811 family connections.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of San Sebastián, established 1752, carved from Aguada. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: San Sebastián was carved from Aguada in 1752 in the western mountains. Known as 'El Pueblo de los Pepineros' or 'El Pepino.' Parroquia San Sebastián Mártir holds records from the mid-18th century. As parent to Lares (1827), pre-1827 Lares records are here. San Sebastián's connection to the Grito de Lares (1868) means military tribunal and political prisoner records at AGPR reference its residents. The hammock-weaving tradition (hamacas) creates artisan records. Coffee and tobacco cultivation generated hacienda records at AGPR. Corsican and Venezuelan immigrants are documented in parish and civil records. Pre-1752 records are in Aguada's ancient parish archives.

source_profiles.source_name: San Sebastián Catholic Parish Registers (Baptisms, Marriages, Burials)
source_profiles.repository: Parish Archives / FamilySearch / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic parish registers for San Sebastián municipio including baptisms (bautismos), marriages (matrimonios), and burials (defunciones). FamilySearch has digitized microfilm for many Puerto Rico parishes. Coverage begins circa 1752.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Parish registers are the primary genealogical source for Puerto Rico. Some early volumes deteriorated due to tropical climate. FamilySearch microfilm provides backup copies.

source_profiles.source_name: San Sebastián Civil Registry (Registro Demográfico) — Births, Marriages, Deaths
source_profiles.repository: Registro Demográfico, San Sebastián / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil registry records for San Sebastián including births, marriages, and deaths from 1885. Spanish civil registration began 1885 under Royal Decree. US administration continued the system after 1898.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Civil registry records generally well-preserved from 1885. Some gaps in early years due to incomplete compliance. Tropical climate and hurricanes have affected some volumes.

source_profiles.source_name: San Sebastián Notarial Protocols (Protocolos Notariales)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Notarial protocols for San Sebastián district covering property transfers, testaments, powers of attorney, marriage contracts, slave sales, coartación agreements, and other legal instruments from 1752. Spanish legal tradition required notarized documents for most transactions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Notarial protocols at AGPR are generally well-preserved. Some volumes from the earliest periods show deterioration. AGPR's climate-controlled storage protects these irreplaceable records.

source_profiles.source_name: San Sebastián Spanish Colonial Census Records (Padrones)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan / Archivo Histórico Nacional, Madrid
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Spanish colonial census records (padrones) for San Sebastián. Include population counts by race, age, occupation, and household. Key padrones: 1765, 1776, 1800, 1812, 1820, 1846, 1860, 1887, 1897. Some padrones list individuals by name; others are statistical summaries.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Survival varies by year. The 1800, 1860, and 1897 padrones are among the most complete. Some early padrones survive only as summaries at the Archivo Histórico Nacional in Madrid.

source_profiles.source_name: San Sebastián Property Registry (Registro de la Propiedad)
source_profiles.repository: Registro de la Propiedad, San Sebastián district
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Property registry records for San Sebastián district. The Spanish mortgage law system (Ley Hipotecaria) was established in Puerto Rico in 1870, creating a formal property registration system that continues today. Records document property ownership, transfers, mortgages, and liens.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Property registry records are generally well-preserved from 1870. The formal registration system provides continuous chains of title.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Municipal cemetery records for San Sebastián. Municipal cemeteries were established in most Puerto Rico municipios during the 19th century under Spanish health regulations. Records include burial registers, grave assignments, and monument inscriptions. Find A Grave has partial crowdsourced transcriptions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Municipal cemetery records vary in completeness. Earlier burials were in church cemeteries (camposantos). Some municipal cemetery records were damaged by hurricanes.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Santa Isabel, established 1842, carved from Coamo. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Santa Isabel was carved from Coamo in 1842 on the south-central coast. Parroquia Santa Isabel Reina de Portugal holds records from the 1840s. Pre-1842 records are in Coamo, one of Puerto Rico's oldest parishes with records from the 1600s. Sugar plantations dominated the coastal economy — Central Cortada and other sugar mills generated extensive employment records. Enslaved persons records are important due to the plantation economy. The coastal plain's agricultural workers came from diverse backgrounds — civil registry records from 1885 document this population. Check Coamo's extensive colonial-era records for pre-separation families. Fishing village records at the coast supplement agricultural records.

source_profiles.source_name: Santa Isabel Slave Registers and Abolition Records (1872-1873)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Slave registers (registros de esclavos) for Santa Isabel including the 1872 census of enslaved persons mandated before the 1873 Abolition Law. Also includes coartación (self-purchase) agreements, liberto (freed persons) records, and slave sale documents in notarial protocols. Essential for tracing African-descended families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: The 1872 slave register is generally well-preserved at AGPR. Earlier slave records are scattered across notarial protocols, church registers, and estate inventories.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Toa Alta, established 1751, one of Puerto Rico's original settlements. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Toa Alta, established as a parish around 1751, sits in the Toa Valley interior south of San Juan. Parroquia San Fernando holds records from the mid-18th century. As parent to Corozal (1795) and co-parent to Morovis, Naranjito, and others, Toa Alta records serve a broad north-central interior area. The Toa Valley was among the earliest areas settled after the San Juan coastal plain. Cattle ranching (hatos) and later sugar cultivation generated hacienda records at AGPR. Spanish colonial padrones from the 1700s document early interior settlement. The Río de la Plata valley settlements predate many other interior municipios. Check notarial protocols for cattle ranch and sugar estate transactions.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Toa Baja, established 1745, one of Puerto Rico's original settlements. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Toa Baja, established as a parish around 1745, is part of the San Juan metropolitan area on the north coast. Parroquia Nuestra Señora de la Concepción holds records from the mid-18th century. As parent to Dorado (1842), pre-1842 Dorado records are here. The Toa Valley was one of Puerto Rico's earliest settled areas — sugar plantations generated extensive enslaved persons records at AGPR. Hacienda Médine and other estates have well-documented histories. Modern industrial development and suburban growth from the 1950s brought population changes — property records at Registro de la Propiedad trace this transformation. Levittown, a major mid-century housing development, created distinctive residential records.
